items.summary: im gonna say its great. Since i play this game before in smartphone. But the ui isn't that good for pc version. You need to touch somewhere first on screen so you can use the space button

items.title: [正面] legitimately just the best dev team of any gatcha;...
items.summary:
legitimately just the best dev team of any gatcha; incredibly f2p friendly (including the pvp mode) and just keeps getting better with each update.

you can't out-spend the game's difficulty, but you don't need to spend a dime to clear all content.  the only gatcha i've ever played where--even after 1000+ hours--i still enjoy the gameplay.

items.title: [正面] The only thing holding this game back for me is th...
items.summary: The only thing holding this game back for me is that it's a gacha indie game. That mean it lack the polishing many gacha games have nowadays. I would love to play this game more but it just not "feel" as good as game like Reverse:1999 or Arknights. This game is a gem for sure, a rough one, but if you can overlook that and go into this game with the mindset that it's an indie game, you can surely have a lot of fun with it.

items.title: [正面] As a long-time gacha player, this is one of my fav...
items.summary: As a long-time gacha player, this is one of my favorite gacha games. It feels like the developers truly care about the game, the story and fair monetization. It deserves every bit of success it gets!

items.title: [正面] Morimens is shockingly good. Too many gacha games ...
items.summary:
Morimens is shockingly good. Too many gacha games focus so heavily on monetization that they forget to be fun. The gameplay here will immediately be familiar to fans of roguelike deckbuilders such as Slay the Spire. The story and theme are compelling, but the translation quality isn't great (and many card descriptions are inconsistent or simply wrong). They're pretty upfront about the game being machine-translated and have started manually correcting the translation now that they have funding so this hopefully won't be an issue in the future. In the first arc, stages involve traversing non-randomized maps with events, relics, shops, battles, and other obstacles as you make your way to the boss. You'll form teams of four Awakeners whose cards are shuffled into a deck. Fights are turn-based and you have a certain amount of energy and cards drawn each turn. In addition, you and your Awakeners can charge up ultimate skills with a wide range of effects. This might seem similar to other turn-based gacha games on paper, but the randomization combined with synergies created both in an out of battle allow you to do all sorts of interesting, "broken" combos from the very start. It feels more like a roguelike deckbuilder with gacha mechanics than a gacha game with roguelike deckbuilder mechanics.

Every Awakener has unique mechanics and cards in addition to being tied to one of four Realms. Chaos Awakeners can blend into other realms without any drawbacks and even some bonuses, while pure Chaos teams refill Keyflare (energy) for your Posse (personal ultimate skill) faster and activate your second Posse in a turn twice. Aequor has tentacles that can be swapped between three stances: the default stance does standard damage and generates a tentacle, the defensive stance shields but does half damage, and the offensive stance deals extra damage but consumes a tentacle. Caro revolves around Embryos and Soul Furnace. Filling up your embryo gauge gives you an embryo that can either grant Aliemus (Awakener ultimate charge) + temporary crit to an Awakener or be Devoured by a Caro Awakener's ultimate for additional effects. Soul Furnace can be consumed to heal and fills up at the end of battle or each turn (with Chaos Awakeners in the team). Ultra has Ultraspace which copies the first card you play each turn up to 5 cards by default. Once full, you can activate an extra turn using whatever cards you had in Ultraspace. Many Ultra characters also have "Leap" effects which apply if they're the first card you used that turn OR on every card during an extra turn. You can alternatively "Annihilate" the leftmost card in Ultraspace to gain an Insight card (1 Arithmetica/energy + 1 draw) for a more rapid-fire playstyle. You can combine characters from any two realms with minor drawbacks (unless the second realm is Chaos, then you get bonuses instead of drawback). This might seem overwhelming at first but the crazy combos you can do in this game are deeply satisfying and fans of the genre won't be disappointed.

Plenty of characters also have interesting mechanics. The very first story character can refund part of the cost of her skills and her ultimate allows you to pick one card from your Draw or Discard pile and place it in your hand with its cost reduced to 0, allowing you to get the perfect card at the perfect moment or just play a crazy card twice in a row. Then you have gacha characters duplicating cards in your hand and creating super embryos (Thais), generating tons of temporary tentacles and buffing them to insane heights (Corposant), decreasing the cost of cards or increasing them to make them play twice (Hameln), pulling cards back out of Ultraspace (Casiah), or flat out generating random cards from any character in your current realm (Tawil). On top of this, the equivalent of equipment called Wheels can also have special effects like giving the character a 60% chance for the cards they play to return to hand, or a chance to reduce the cost of cards in your hand when they Exalt (ultimate). And then on top of this, you have Relics during runs with effects like "every 8th card takes effect twice" along with Orisons which modify specific cards for the run with effects like "gain 3 Strength when played" or "takes effect twice but is Exhausted for the battle". All these interesting gameplay mechanics come together in ways that make me feel like I'm playing a well-crafted indie roguelike deckbuilder rather than a soul-sucking gacha game.

As for the gacha side... It's pretty generous for a gacha game. You'll get tons of Standard rolls and you choose a realm when rolling for standard Awakeners (Aequor and Chaos are the easiest to start with). About a week in, I've gotten 475 standard rolls and 175 limited rolls between the 1.5th anniversary events and first clear bonuses up through Chapter 4 normal (not even half way into the story). According to another player, free players can expect 50+ limited rolls a month. SSR rate is 3.03% with a guaranteed SSR at 30 pulls. There are only 5 standard SSRs in each realm so your roster will be padded out in no time. Limited characters are eventually added to the standard banner at a lower rate. Limited Awakener banners have a 33% chance to be pulled with pity after failing twice (90 rolls max). The Standard Wheels of Destiny (equipment) banner allows you to target any standard SSR wheel, also 33% chance with pity after failing twice. Limited Wheel banners are 50% chance with pity after one fail (60 rolls max). Limited Wheels are added to the standard banner alongside their characters at a lower rate and no targeting. In addition, character rebalances give items that allow you to remove all the duplicates from a character and convert them to standard Awakener selectors. You also get one universal dupe item for Awakeners and one for Wheels each month so you can slowly build up anything as long as you have one copy. The tradeoff here is that most Awakeners need duplicates to unlock their full kit: 3 duplicates to unlock all passive effects, 7 duplicates to unlock their "overexalt" (super ultimate using 200% charge), and 15 duplicates to show off. Most characters function fine without overexalt, so you only need 1-4 copies depending on the character. Wheels unlock their full passives with 3 duplicates and allow you to equip another SSR wheel with 15 duplicates. You can just use a maxed out farmable SSR wheel to skirt around this or even an SR wheel and you'll be fine if you aren't trying to tackle the hardest content. Free players should be able to get at least one copy of whatever they want and slowly build them up each month, as long as you don't roll for everything.

Another important aspect of gacha games is time investment. Only farming stages and event stages use stamina in this game, while story stages can be done at your own pace. After clearing a stage once, you can sweep it to spend stamina and instantly get the rewards so farming events beyond the first run is incredibly easy. Daily tasks will be completed just by sweeping event stages (better use of stamina than the farming stages) and sending your characters out to get materials each day. The PvP daily is shared with a PvE mode called Phantasmal Dive so you only have to do one or the other and neither takes more than a few minutes, plus you get 2 skip tickets for Phantasmal Dive each week. The true endgame mode is called D-effect Zone (D-tide) which requires 5 separate teams (20 characters) and resets every few weeks. Morimens is a great side game since it won't take much of your time, and it's also a solid main game since the gameplay and story (translations aside) are compelling.

tl;dr excellent gameplay mechanics and visuals, generous gacha mechanics (but still gacha so beware), solid story with iffy translations
I know this review is on the longer side but it can be difficult to find basic info about this game so I wanted to provide some guidance beyond a thumbs up.
